Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


000
001
      C ERTIFIED D EFENSE ON THE FAIRNESS OF
002   G RAPH N EURAL N ETWORKS
003
004
005    Anonymous authors
006    Paper under double-blind review
007
008
009                                                A BSTRACT
010
011            Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have emerged as a prominent graph learning
012            model in various graph-based tasks over the years. Nevertheless, due to the vulner-
013            abilities of GNNs, it has been empirically proved that malicious attackers could
014            easily corrupt the fairness level of their predictions by adding perturbations to the
015            input graph data. In this paper, we take crucial steps to study a novel problem
016            of certifiable defense on the fairness level of GNNs. Specifically, we propose a
017            principled framework named ELEGANT and present a detailed theoretical cer-
018
               tification analysis for the fairness of GNNs. ELEGANT takes any GNNs as its
               backbone, and the fairness level of such a backbone is theoretically impossible to
019
               be corrupted under certain perturbation budgets for attackers. Notably, ELEGANT
020
               does not have any assumption over the GNN structure or parameters, and does
021            not require re-training the GNNs to realize certification. Hence it can serve as
022            a plug-and-play framework for any optimized GNNs ready to be deployed. We
023            verify the satisfactory effectiveness of ELEGANT in practice through extensive
024            experiments on real-world datasets across different backbones of GNNs, where
025            ELEGANT is also demonstrated to be beneficial for GNN debiasing.
026
027
028   1    I NTRODUCTION
029
030   Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have emerged among the most popular models to handle learning
031   tasks on graphs (Kipf & Welling, 2017; Veličković et al., 2018) and made remarkable achievements in
032   various domains (Feng et al., 2022; Li et al., 2022). Nevertheless, as GNNs are increasingly deployed
033   in real-world decision-making scenarios, there has been an increasing societal concern on the fairness
034   of GNN predictions. A primary reason is that most traditional GNNs do not consider fairness, and
035
      thus could exhibit bias against certain demographic subgroups. Here the demographic subgroups are
036
      usually divided by certain sensitive attributes, such as gender and race. To prevent GNNs from biased
      predictions, multiple recent studies have proposed fairness-aware GNNs (Agarwal et al., 2021; Dai &
037
      Wang, 2021; Kang et al., 2022a) such that potential bias could be mitigated.
038
039   Unfortunately, despite existing efforts towards fair GNNs, it remains difficult to prevent the corruption
040   of their fairness level due to their common vulnerability of lacking adversarial robustness. In fact,
041   malicious attackers can easily corrupt the fairness level of GNNs by perturbing the node attributes
042   (i.e., changing the values of node attributes) and/or the graph structure (i.e., adding and deleting
043   edges) (Hussain et al., 2022), which could lead to serious consequences in the test phase (Dai
044   & Wang, 2021; Hussain et al., 2022). For example, GNNs have been leveraged to perform bail
045   decision-making on the graph of defendants, where an edge between two defendants represents high
046
      profile similarity (Agarwal et al., 2021). Yet, by simply injecting adversarial links in the graph data,
      attackers can make GNNs deliver advantaged predictions for a subgroup (e.g., individuals with a
047
      certain nationality) while damaging the interest of others (Hussain et al., 2022). Hence achieving
048
      defense over the fairness of GNNs is crucial for the purpose of safe deployment.
049
050   It is worth noting that despite the abundant empirical defense strategies for GNNs (Zhang & Zitnik,
051   2020; Entezari et al., 2020; Jin & Zhang, 2019; Jin et al., 2020c; Wu et al., 2019b), they are always
052   subsequently defeated by novel attacking techniques (Schuchardt et al., 2020; Carlini & Wagner,
053   2017), and the defense over the fairness of GNNs also faces the same problem. Therefore, an
      ideal way is to achieve certifiable defense on fairness (i.e., certified fairness defense). A few recent


                                                         1
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


054
      works aim to certify the fairness for traditional deep learning models (Khedr & Shoukry, 2022;
055
      Kang et al., 2022b; Jin et al., 2022; Mangold et al., 2022; Borca-Tasciuc et al., 2022; Ruoss et al.,
056   2020). Nevertheless, most of them require specially designed training strategies (Khedr & Shoukry,
057   2022; Jin et al., 2022; Ruoss et al., 2020) and thus cannot be directly applied to optimized GNNs
058   ready to be deployed. More importantly, they mostly rely on assumptions on the optimization
059   results (Khedr & Shoukry, 2022; Jin et al., 2022; Borca-Tasciuc et al., 2022; Ruoss et al., 2020) or
060   data distributions (Kang et al., 2022b; Mangold et al., 2022) over a continuous input space. Hence
061   they can hardly be generalized to GNNs due to the binary nature of the input graph topology. Several
062   other works propose certifiable GNN defense approaches to achieve theoretical guarantee (Wang
063   et al., 2021; Bojchevski & Günnemann, 2019; Bojchevski et al., 2020; Jin et al., 2020a; Zügner &
064   Günnemann, 2019; 2020). However, they mainly focus on securing the GNN prediction for a certain
065   individual node to ensure model utility, ignoring the fairness defense over the entire population.
066   Therefore, despite the significance, the study in this field still remains in its infancy.
067   It is worth noting that achieving certifiable defense on the fairness of GNNs is a daunting task
068   due to the following key challenges: (1) Generality: different types of GNNs could be designed
069   and optimized for different real-world applications (Zhou et al., 2020). Correspondingly, our first
070   challenge is to design a plug-and-play framework that can achieve certified defense on fairness for
071   any optimized GNN models that are ready to be deployed. (2) Vulnerability: a plethora of existing
072   studies have empirically verified that most GNNs are sensitive to input data perturbations (Zhang &
073   Zitnik, 2020; Zügner et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2019). In other words, small input perturbations may
074   cause significant changes in the GNN output. Hence our second challenge is to properly mitigate the
075   common vulnerabilities of GNNs without changing its structure or re-training. (3) Multi-Modality:
076   the input data of GNNs naturally bears multiple modalities. For example, there are node attributes
077   and graph topology in the widely studied attributed networks. In practice, both data modalities may
078   be perturbed by malicious attackers. Therefore, our third challenge is to achieve certified defenses of
079   fairness on both data modalities for GNNs.
080   As an early attempt to address the aforementioned challenges, in this paper, we propose a principled
081   framework named ELEGANT (cEtifiabLE GNNs over the fAirNess of PredicTions). Specifically,
082   we focus on the widely studied task of node classification and formulate a novel research problem
083   of Certifying GNN Classifiers on Fairness. To handle the first challenge, we propose to develop
084   ELEGANT on top of an optimized GNN model without any assumptions over its structure or
085   parameters. Hence ELEGANT is able to serve as a plug-and-play framework for any optimized GNN
086   model ready to be deployed. To handle the second challenge, we propose to leverage randomized
087   smoothing (Wang et al., 2021; Cohen et al., 2019) to defend against malicious attacks, where most
088   GNNs can then be more robust over the prediction fairness level. To handle the third challenge, we
089   propose two different strategies working in a concurrent manner, such that certified defense against
090   the attacks on both the node attributes (i.e., add and subtract attribute values) and graph topology
091
      (i.e., flip the existence of edges) can be realized. Finally, we evaluate the effectiveness of ELEGANT
092
      on multiple real-world network datasets. In summary, our contributions are three-fold: (1) Problem
      Formulation. We formulate and make an initial investigation on a novel research problem of
093
      Certifying GNN Classifiers on Fairness. (2) Algorithm Design. We propose a framework ELEGANT
094
      to achieve certified fairness defense against attacks on both node attributes and graph structure
095
      without relying on assumptions about any specific GNNs. (3) Experimental Evaluation. We
096
      perform comprehensive experiments on real-world datasets to verify the effectiveness of ELEGANT.
097
098
099   2    P ROBLEM D EFINITION
100
101   Preliminaries. Let G = {V, E} be an undirected attributed network, where V = {v1 , ..., vn } is the
102   set of n nodes; E ⊆ V × V is the set of edges. Let A ∈ {0, 1}n×n and X ∈ Rn×d be the adjacency
103   matrix and attribute matrix of G, respectively. Assume each node in G represents an individual,
104   and sensitive attribute s divides the population into different demographic subgroups. We follow a
105   widely studied setting (Agarwal et al., 2021; Dai & Wang, 2021) to assume the sensitive attribute is
106   binary, i.e., s ∈ {0, 1}. We use si to denote the value of the sensitive attribute for node vi . In node
107   classification tasks, we use Vtrn and Vtst (Vtrn , Vtst ∈ V) to represent the training and test node set,
      respectively. We denote the GNN node classifier as fθ parameterized by θ. fθ takes A and X as


                                                         2
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


108
      input, and outputs Ŷ as the predictions for the nodes in G. Each row in Ŷ is a one-hot vector flagging
109
      the predicted class. We use fθ∗ to denote the GNN with optimal parameter θ ∗ .
110
111   Threat Model. We focus on the attacking scenario of model evasion, i.e., the attack happens
112   in the test phase. In particular, we assume that the victim model under attack is an optimized
113   GNN node classifier fθ∗ . We follow a widely adopted setting (Bojchevski & Günnemann, 2019;
114
      Zügner & Günnemann, 2019; Ma et al., 2020; Mu et al., 2021) to assume that a subset of nodes
115
      Vvul ∈ Vtst are vulnerable to attacks. Specifically, attackers may perturb their links (i.e., flip the
      edge existence) to other nodes and/or their node attributes (i.e., change their attribute values). We
116
      denote the perturbations on adjacency matrix as A ⊕ ∆A . Here ⊕ denotes the element-wise XOR
117
      operator; ∆A ∈ {0, 1}n×n is the matrix representing the perturbations made by the attacker, where 1
118
      only appears in rows and columns associated with the vulnerable nodes while 0 appears elsewhere.
119
      Correspondingly, in ∆A , 1 entries represent edges that attackers intend to flip, while 0 entries are
120   associated with edges that are not attacked. Similarly, we denote the perturbations on node attribute
121   matrix as X + ∆X , where ∆X ∈ Rn×n is the matrix representing the perturbations made by the
122   attacker. Usually, if the total magnitude of perturbations is within certain budgets (i.e., ∥∆A ∥0 ≤ ϵA
123   for A and ∥∆X ∥2 ≤ ϵX for X), the perturbations are regarded as unnoticeable. The goal of an
124   attacker is to add unnoticeable perturbations to nodes in Vvul , such that the GNN predictions for nodes
125   in Vtst based on the perturbed graph exhibit as much bias as possible. In addition, we assume that the
126   attacker has access to any information about the victim GNN (i.e., a white-box setting). This is the
127   worst case in practice, which makes it even more challenging to achieve defense.
128
      To defend against the aforementioned attacks, we aim to establish a node classifier on top of an
129
      optimized GNN backbone, such that this classifier, theoretically, will not exhibit more bias than a
130
      given threshold no matter what unnoticeable perturbations (i.e., perturbations within budgets) are
131   added. We formally formulate the problem of Certifying GNN Classifiers on Fairness below.
132
133
      Problem 1. Certifying GNN Classifiers on Fairness. Given an attributed network G, a test node set
134
      Vtst , a vulnerable node set Vvul ∈ Vtst , a threshold η for the exhibited bias, and an optimized GNN
      classifier fθ∗ , our goal is to achieve a classifier on top of fθ∗ associated with budgets ϵA and ϵX ,
135
      such that this classifier will bear comparable utility with fθ∗ but provably not exhibit more bias
136
      than η on the nodes in Vtst , no matter what unnoticeable node attributes and/or graph structure
137
      perturbations (i.e., perturbations within budgets) are made over the nodes in Vvul .
138
139
140   3     M ETHODOLOGY
141
142   Here we first introduce the modeling of attack and defense on the fairness of GNNs, then discuss how
143   we achieve certified defense on node attributes. After that, we propose a strategy to achieve both types
144   of certified defense (i.e., defense on node attributes and graph structure) at the same time. Finally, we
145   introduce strategies to achieve the designed certified fairness defense for GNNs in practice.
146
147   3.1   B IAS I NDICATOR F UNCTION
148
149   We first construct an indicator g to mathematically model the attack and defense on the fairness of
150   GNNs. Our rationale is to use g to indicate whether the predictions of fθ∗ exhibit a level of bias
151   exceeding a given threshold. We present the formal definition below.
152   Definition 1. (Bias Indicator Function) Given adjacency matrix A and node attribute matrix X,
153   a test node set Vtst , a threshold η for the exhibited bias, and an optimized GNN model fθ∗ , the
154   bias indicator function is defined as g(fθ∗ , A, X, η, Vtst ) = 1 (π(fθ∗ (A, X), Vtst ) < η), where 1(·)
155   takes an event as input and outputs 1 if the event happens (otherwise 0); π(·, ·) denotes any bias
156   metric for GNN predictions (taken as its first parameter) over a set of nodes (taken as its second
157   parameter). Traditional bias metrics include ∆SP (Dai & Wang, 2021; Dwork et al., 2012) and
158   ∆EO (Dai & Wang, 2021; Hardt et al., 2016).
159
160   Correspondingly, the goal of the attacker is to ensure that the indicator g outputs 0 for an η as large
161   as possible, while the goal of certified defense is to ensure for a given threshold η, the indicator g
      provably yields 1 as long as the attacks are within certain budgets. Note that a reasonable η should


                                                         3
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


162
      ensure that g outputs 1 based on the clean graph data (i.e., graph data without any attacks). Below we
163
      first discuss the certified fairness defense over node attributes to maintain the output of g as 1.
164
165
      3.2   C ERTIFIED FAIRNESS D EFENSE OVER N ODE ATTRIBUTES
166
167   We now introduce how we achieve certified defense over the node attributes for the fairness of the
168   predictions yielded by fθ∗ . Specifically, we propose to construct a smoothed bias indicator function
169   g̃X (fθ∗ , A, X, Vvul , η) via adding Gaussian noise over the node attributes of vulnerable nodes in
170   Vvul . For simplicity, we use g̃X (A, X) to represent the smoothed bias indicator function over node
171   attributes by omitting Vvul , fθ∗ and η. We formally define g̃X below.
172
      Definition 2. (Bias Indicator with Node Attribute Smoothing) We define the bias indicator with
173   smoothed node attributes over the nodes in Vvul as g̃X (A, X) = argmaxc∈{0,1} Pr(g(fθ∗ , A, X +
174
      γX (ωX , Vvul ), η, Vtst ) = c). Here ωX is a (d · |Vvul |)-dimensional vector, where each entry is
175   a random variable following a Gaussian Distribution N (0, σ 2 ); γX (·, ·) maps a vector (its first
176   parameter) to an (n × d)-dimensional matrix, where the vector values are assigned to rows with the
177   indices indicated by a set of nodes (its second parameter) while other entries are zeros.
178
179   We denote ΓX = γX (ωX , Vvul ) and g(A, X + ΓX ) = g(fθ∗ , A, X + γX (ωX , Vvul ), η, Vtst ) below
180   for simplicity. We are then able to derive the theoretical certification for the defense on fairness with
181   the defined g̃X in Definition 2. We now present the defense certification on fairness below.
182   Theorem 1. (Certified Fairness Defense for Node Attributes) Denote the probability for g(A, X +
183   ΓX ) to return class c (c ∈ {0, 1}) as P (c). Then g̃X (A, X) will provably return argmaxc∈{0,1} P (c)
184   for any perturbations (over the attributes of vulnerable          nodes) within an l2 radius ϵ˜X =
           −1                           −1
      σ
                                                                        Φ−1 (·) is the inverse of the standard

185
       2 Φ    (max  c∈{0,1} P  (c)) − Φ    (min c∈{0,1} P (c))  , where
186   Gaussian cumulative distribution function.
187
188   Correspondingly, for an η that enables maxc∈{0,1} P (c) = 1, it is then safe to say that no matter
189   what perturbations ∆X are made on vulnerable nodes, as long as ∥∆X ∥2 ≤ ϵ̃X , the constructed
190   g̃X will provably not yield predictions for Vtst with a level of bias exceeding η. Nevertheless, it is
191
      worth noting that, in GNNs, perturbations may also be made on the structure of the vulnerable nodes,
192
      i.e., adding and/or deleting edges between these vulnerable nodes and any nodes in the graph. Hence
      it is also necessary to achieve certified defense against such structural attacks. Here we propose to
193
      also smooth the constructed g̃X over the graph structure (of the vulnerable nodes) for the purpose of
194
      certified fairness defense on the graph structure. However, the adjacency matrix describing the graph
195
      structure is naturally binary, and thus should be smoothed in a different way.
196
197
      3.3   C ERTIFIED FAIRNESS D EFENSE OVER N ODE ATTRIBUTES AND G RAPH S TRUCTURE
198
199   We then introduce achieving certified fairness defense against attacks on both node attributes and
200   graph structure. We propose a strategy to leverage noise following Bernoulli distribution to smooth
201   g̃X over the rows and columns (due to symmetricity) associated with the vulnerable nodes in A. In
202   this way, we can smooth both the node attributes and graph structure for g in a randomized manner,
203   and we denote the constructed function as g̃A,X . We present the formal definition below.
204
      Definition 3. (Bias Indicator with Attribute-Structure Smoothing) We define the bias indicator
205
      function with smoothed node attributes and graph structure over the nodes in Vvul as g̃A,X (A, X) =
206
      argmaxc∈{0,1} Pr(g̃X (A ⊕ γA (ωA , Vvul ), X) = c). Here ωA is an (n · |Vvul |)-dimensional random
207   variable, where each dimension takes 0 and 1 with the probability of β (0.5 < β ≤ 1) and 1 − β,
208   respectively; function γA (·, ·) maps a vector (its first parameter) to a symmetric (n × n)-dimensional
209   matrix, where the vector values are assigned to rows whose indices associated with the indices of
210   a set of nodes (its second parameter) and then mirrored to the corresponding columns, while other
211   values are left as zeros.
212
213   We let ΓA = γA (ωA , Vvul ) below for simplicity. To better illustrate how classifier g̃A,X achieves
214   certified fairness defense over both data modalities of an attributed network, we provide an ex-
215   emplary case in Figure 1. Here we assume node vi ∈ Vvul . Considering the high dimen-
      sionality of node attributes and adjacency matrix, we only analyze two entries Xi,j and Ai,j


                                                         4
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


216
      and omit other entries after noise for simplicity. Here the superscript (i, j) represents the i-th
217
      row and j-th column of a matrix. Under binary noise, entry Ai,j only has two possible val-
218   ues, i.e., Ai,j ⊕ 0 and Ai,j ⊕ 1. We denote the two cases as Case (1) and Case (2), respec-
219   tively. We assume that the area where g returns 1 in the span of the two input random entries
220   of g (i.e., Xi,j and Ai,j under random noise) is an ellipse (marked out with green), where
221   the decision boundary is marked out with deep green. In Case (1), Xi,j under random noise
222   follows a Gaussian distribution, whose probability density function is marked out as deep red.
223                                                 We assume that, in this case, the integral of the proba-

242
      Lemma 1. (Perturbation-Invariant Budgets Existence) There exist tractable budgets ϵA and ϵX ,
243
      such that for any perturbations made over the node attributes and graph structure of the vulnerable
244
      nodes within ϵA and ϵX , g̃A,X provably maintains the same classification results.
245
246
247   Correspondingly, for an η that enables g̃A,X to return 1, we are then able to achieve certified fairness
248   defense over g̃A,X against perturbations on both node attributes and graph structure. Below we derive
249   the certified fairness defense budgets over the graph structure ϵA and node attributes ϵX for g̃A,X .
250
      We first introduce the derivation of ϵA . Here, our rationale is: considering that g̃A,X is a binary
251
      classifier, we need to ensure that under structure attacks, the probability of g̃X returning 1 (denoted
      as Pr(g̃X (A ⊕ ∆A ⊕ ΓA , X) = 1)) is provably greater than 0.5, such that g̃A,X will still return
252
      1. To this end, we propose to derive a lower bound of Pr(g̃X (A ⊕ ∆A ⊕ ΓA , X) = 1), which we
253
      denote as Pg̃X =1 . Finally, we identify the largest perturbation size that keeps such a lower bound
254
      larger than 0.5, and the identified perturbation size is then the graph structure perturbation budget.
255
      We present the lower bound of Pr(g̃X (A ⊕ ∆A ⊕ ΓA , X) = 1) below.
256
257   Lemma 2. (Positive Probability Bound Under Noises) There exists a tractable Pg̃X =1 ∈ (0, 1), such
258
      that Pr(g̃X (A ⊕ ∆A ⊕ ΓA , X) = 1) ≥ Pg̃X =1 .
259
260
261   To derive the perturbation budget ϵA , we only need to find a ∆A with the largest l0 -norm that still
262   enables Pg̃X =1 to be greater than 0.5 (according to Definition 3). Correspondingly, we derive the
263   theoretical perturbation-invariant budget ϵA in Theorem 2 below.
264
265
      Theorem 2. (Certified Defense Budget for Structure Perturbations) The certified defense budget over
266
      the graph structure ϵA for g̃A,X is given as
                              ϵA = max ϵ˜A , s.t. Pg̃X =1 > 0.5, ∀ ∥∆A ∥0 ≤ ϵ˜A .                     (1)
267
268
269   To solve the optimization problem in Equation (1), we introduce Theorem 3 to compute Pg̃X =1 .

287
288   3.4   C ERTIFICATION IN P RACTICE
289
      Estimating the Predicted Label Probabilities. According to Definition 3, it is necessary to obtain
290
      Pr(g̃X (A ⊕ ΓA , X) = c) (c ∈ {0, 1}) to determine the output of classifier g̃X . We propose
291
      to leverage a Monte Carlo method to estimate such a probability. Specifically, we first randomly
292
      pick N samples of ΓA as Ā′ (Ā′ ⊂ Ā). Considering the output of g̃X is binary, we then follow
293   a common strategy (Cohen et al., 2019) to consider this problem as a parameter estimation of a
294   Binomial distribution: we first count the number of returned label 1 and 0 under noise as N1 and
295   N0 (N1 + N0 = N ); then we choose a confidence level 1 − α and take the α-th quantile of the beta
296   distribution with parameters N1 and N0 as the estimated probability lower bound for returning label
297   c = 1. We proved that all theoretical analysis still holds true for such an estimation in Appendix. We
298   follow a similar strategy to estimate the probability lower bound of yielding 1 for g(A, X + ΓX ).
299
      Obtaining Fair Classification Results. After achieving certified fairness defense based on g̃A,X , we
300
      also need to obtain the corresponding node classification results (given by fθ∗ ) over Vtst . We propose
301
      to collect all classification results associated with the sampled Γ′A ∈ Ā′ that leads to an estimated
302
      lower bound of Pr(g̃X (A ⊕ Γ′A , X) = 1) to be larger than 0.5 as Ŷ ′ . Here Ŷ ′ is a set of output
303
      matrices of fθ∗ , where each matrix consists of the one-hot output classification results (as each row
304
      in the matrix) for all nodes. We propose to take argminŶ ′ π(Ŷ ′ , Vtst ), s.t. Ŷ ′ ∈ Ŷ ′ as the final node
305
      classification results. Correspondingly, consider Pr(g̃X (A ⊕ Γ′A , X) = 1) falls into the confidence
306
      interval characterized by 1 − α, we have a neat probabilistic theoretical guarantee below.
307
308   Proposition 1. (Probabilistic Guarantee for the Fairness Level of Node Classification). For Ŷ =
                                                                                           ′
309   argminŶ ′ π(Ŷ ′ , Vtst ), s.t. Ŷ ′ ∈ Ŷ ′ , we have Pr(π(Ŷ , Vtst ) > η) < 0.5|Ŷ | .
310
311   Note that for a large enough sample size N , the cardinality of Ŷ ′ also tends to be large in practice.
312   Hence it is safe to argue that Pr(π(Ŷ , Vtst ) > η) tends to be small enough. In other words, we have a
313   probability that is large enough to obtain results with a bias level lower than threshold η.
314
      Calculation of Perturbation Budgets. We calculate ϵA by solving the optimization problem
315
      given in Equation (1), and we provide the completed procedure in Appendix. For ϵX , we uti-
316
      lize a Monte Carlo method to estimate its value. More specifically, we leverage min{ϵ˜X :
317   ϵ˜X is derived with classifier g̃X (A ⊕ Γ′A , X) , where Γ′A ∈ Ā′ } to estimate the value of ϵX .
318
319
320   4     E XPERIMENTAL E VALUATIONS
321
322   In this section, we aim to answer three research questions: RQ1: How well does ELEGANT perform
323   in achieving certified fairness defense? RQ2: How does ELEGANT perform under fairness attacks
      compared to other popular fairness-aware GNNs? RQ3: How does ELEGANT perform under


                                                            6
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


324
      different settings of parameters? We present the main experimental settings and representative results
325
      in this section due to space limits. Detailed settings and supplementary experiments are in Appendix.
326
327
      4.1   E XPERIMENTAL S ETTINGS
328
329
      Downstream Task and Datasets. We focus on the widely studied node classification task, which is
330   one of the most representative tasks in the domain of learning on graphs. We adopt three real-world
331   network datasets that are widely used to perform studies on the fairness of GNNs, namely German
332   Credit (Agarwal et al., 2021; Asuncion & Newman, 2007), Recidivism (Agarwal et al., 2021; Jordan
333   & Freiburger, 2015), and Credit Defaulter (Agarwal et al., 2021; Yeh & Lien, 2009). We provide
334   their basic information, including how these datasets are built and their statistics, in Appendix.
335
      Evaluation Metrics. We perform evaluation from three main perspectives, including model utility,
336
      fairness, and certified defense. To evaluate utility, we adopt the node classification accuracy. To
337
      evaluate fairness, we adopt the widely used metrics ∆SP (measuring bias under Statistical Parity) and
338
      ∆EO (measuring bias under Equal Opportunity). To evaluate certified defense, we extend a traditional
339
      metric named Certified Accuracy (Wang et al., 2021; Cohen et al., 2019) in our experiments, and we
340
      name it as Fairness Certification Rate (FCR). Specifically, existing GNN certification works mainly
341   focus on a certain individual node, and utilize certified accuracy to measure the ratio of nodes that are
342   correctly classified and also successfully certified out of all test nodes (Wang et al., 2021). In this
343   paper, however, we perform certified (fairness) defense for individuals over an entire test set (instead
344   of for any specific individual). Accordingly, we propose to sample multiple test sets out of nodes that
345   are not involved in the training and validation set. Then we perform certified fairness defense for
346   all sampled test sets, and utilize the ratio of test sets that are successfully certified over all sampled
347   sets as the metric of certified defense. The rationale of FCR is leveraging a Monte Carlo method to
348   estimate the probability of being successfully certified for a randomly sampled test node set.
349
      GNN Backbones and Baselines. Note that ELEGANT serves as a plug-and-play framework for
350
      any optimized GNNs ready to be deployed. To evaluate the generality of ELEGANT across GNNs,
351   we adopt three of the most representative GNNs spanning across simple and complex ones, namely
352   Graph Sample and Aggregate Networks (Hamilton et al., 2017) (GraphSAGE), Graph Convolutional
353   Networks (Kipf & Welling, 2017) (GCN), and Jumping Knowledge Networks (JK). Note that to the
354   best of our knowledge, existing works on fairness certification cannot certify the attacks over two
355   data modalities (i.e., continuous node attributes and binary graph topology) at the same time, and
356   thus cannot be naively generalized onto GNNs. Hence we compare the usability of GNNs before and
357   after certification with ELEGANT. Moreover, we also adopt two popular fairness-aware GNNs as
358   baselines to evaluate bias mitigation, including FairGNN (Dai & Wang, 2021) and NIFTY (Agarwal
359   et al., 2021). Specifically, FairGNN utilizes adversarial learning to debias node embeddings, while
360   NIFTY designs regularizations to debias node embeddings.
361
      Threat Models. We propose to evaluate the performance of ELEGANT and other fairness-aware
362   GNN models under actual attacks on fairness. We first introduce the threat model over graph structure.
363   To the best of our knowledge, FA-GNN (Hussain et al., 2022) is the only work that performs graph
364   structure attacks targeting the fairness of GNNs. Hence we adopt FA-GNN to attack graph structure.
365   In terms of node attributes, to the best of our knowledge, no existing work has made any explorations.
366   Hence we directly utilize gradient ascend to perform attacks. Specifically, after structure attacks have
367   been performed, we identify the top-ranked node attribute elements (out of the node attribute matrix)
368   that positively influence the exhibited bias the most via gradient ascend. For any given budget (of
369   attacks) on node attributes, we add perturbations to these elements in proportion to their gradients.
370
371   4.2   RQ1: FAIRNESS C ERTIFICATION E FFECTIVENESS
372
373   To answer RQ1, we investigate the performance of different GNNs after certification across different
374   real-world attributed network datasets over FCR, utility, and fairness. We present the experimental
375   results across three GNN backbones and three real-world attributed network datasets in Table 1.
376   Here bias is measured with ∆SP , and we have similar observations on ∆EO . We summarize the main
377   observations as follows: (1) Fairness Certification Rate (FCR). We observe that ELEGANT realizes
      values of FCR around or even higher than 90% for all three GNN backbones and three attributed


                                                          7
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025

381   the opposite. Numerical values are in percentage, and the best ones are in bold.

393
394
395
396   network datasets, especially for the German Credit dataset, where vanilla GNNs tend to exhibit a high
397   level of bias. The corresponding intuition is that, for nodes in any randomly sampled test set, we have
398   a probability around or higher than 90% to successfully certify the fairness level of the predictions
399   yielded by the GNN model with our proposed framework ELEGANT. Hence ELEGANT achieves a
400   satisfying fairness certification rate across all adopted GNN backbones and datasets. (2) Utility. We
401   found that compared with those vanilla GNN backbones, certified GNNs with ELEGANT also exhibit
402
      comparable and even higher node classification accuracy values in all cases. Hence we conclude that
403
      our proposed framework ELEGANT does not significantly jeopardize the utility of the vanilla GNN
      models, and those certified GNNs with ELEGANT still bear a high level of usability in terms of
404
      node classification accuracy. (3) Fairness. Although the goal of ELEGANT is not debiasing GNNs,
405
      we observe that certified GNNs with ELEGANT achieve better performances in all cases in terms
406
      of algorithmic fairness compared with those vanilla GNNs. This demonstrates that the proposed
407
      framework ELEGANT also contributes to bias mitigation. We conjecture that such an advantage of
408   debiasing could be a mixed result of (1) adding random noise on node attributes and graph topology
409   (as in Section 3.2 and Section 3.3) and (2) the proposed strategy of obtaining fair classification results
410   (as in Section 3.4). We provide a more detailed analysis in Appendix B.9.
411
412
413   4.3     RQ2: FAIRNESS C ERTIFICATION UNDER ATTACKS
414
415   To answer RQ2, we perform attacks on the fairness of GCN, E-GCN, FairGNN (with a GCN back-
416   bone), and NIFTY (with a GCN backbone). Considering the large size of the quadratic space spanned
417   by the sizes of perturbations ∆A and ∆X , we present the evaluation under four representative
418   (∥∆A ∥0 , ∥∆X ∥2 ) pairs. We set the threshold for bias η to be 50% higher than the fairness level of
419   the vanilla GCN model on clean data, since it empirically helps to achieve a high certification success
420   rate under large perturbations.
421   We present the fairness levels of the four models in terms of ∆EO in Figure 2. Note that we utilize
422   a vanilla GCN to predict the labels for test nodes to obtain the classification results discussed in
423   Section 3.4, and we also have similar observations on other GNNs/datasets. (1) Fairness. We found
424   that the GCN model with the proposed framework ELEGANT achieves the lowest level of bias in
425   all cases of fairness attacks. This observation is consistent with the superiority in fairness found
426   in Table 1, which demonstrates that the fairness superiority of ELEGANT maintains even under
427   attacks within a wide range of attacking perturbation sizes. (2) Certification on Fairness. We now
428   compare the performance of E-GCN across different attacking perturbation sizes. We observed
429   that under relatively small attacking perturbation sizes, i.e., (20 , 10−1 ), (21 , 100 ), and (22 , 101 ),
430   ELEGANT successfully achieves certification over fairness, and the bias level increases slowly as the
431   size of attacks increases. Under relatively large attacking perturbation size, i.e., (23 , 102 ), although
      the attacking budgets go beyond the certified budgets, GCN under ELEGANT still exhibits a fairness

      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


486
      (1) if the primary goal is to achieve certification with a high probability, then larger values for β (e.g.,
487
      0.8 and 0.9) would be preferred; (2) if the goal is to achieve certification with larger certified defense
488   budgets on the graph topology, then smaller values for β (e.g., 0.6 and 0.7) should be selected.
489
490
491
      5    R ELATED W ORK
492
493
494   Algorithmic Fairness in GNNs. Existing GNN works on fairness mainly focus on group fairness and
495   individual fairness (Dong et al., 2022b). Specifically, group fairness requires that each demographic
496
      subgroup (divided by sensitive attributes such as gender and race) in the graph should have their fair
497
      share of interest based on predictions (M. et al., 2021). Adversarial training is among the most popular
      strategies (Dai & Wang, 2021; Dong et al., 2022b). In addition, regularization (Agarwal et al., 2021;
498
      Fan et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2021), topology modification (Dong et al., 2022a; Spinelli et al., 2021),
499
      and orthogonal projection (Palowitch & Perozzi, 2020) are also commonly used strategies. On the
500
      other hand, individual fairness it requires that similar individuals should be treated similarly (Dwork
501
      et al., 2012), where such similarity may be determined in different ways (Kang et al., 2020; Dong
502
      et al., 2021). Designing optimization regularization terms to promote individual fairness for GNNs is
503   a common strategy (Fan et al., 2021; Dong et al., 2021; Song et al., 2022). Nevertheless, despite the
504   research advancements in the field of algorithmic fairness on GNNs, the adversarial defense against
505   fairness attacks still remains in its infancy and has not been thoroughly explored. To the best of our
506   knowledge, our paper serves as the first comprehensive study dedicated to addressing this important
507   research problem, paving the way for future investigations in this under-explored area.
508
      GNN Defense Against Attacks. Existing works on GNN defense are mainly categorized into four
509
      mainstreams, namely adversarial training (Xu et al., 2019; Dai et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2019),
510
      graph data purification (Entezari et al., 2020; Jin et al., 2020c; Wu et al., 2019a; Kipf & Welling,
511
      2016), perturbation detection (Xu et al., 2018; Ioannidis et al., 2019; Jin et al., 2020b), and certified
512
      defense (Schuchardt et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021; Bojchevski & Günnemann, 2019; Zügner &
513   Günnemann, 2020; Jia et al., 2020). Adversarial training aims to inject adversarial examples (e.g.,
514   edges) during training, such that the GNN tends to yield correct predictions for adversarial examples
515   during inference (Xu et al., 2019; Dai et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2019). Graph data purification
516   also works during training, where graph data is purified during learning to weaken the influence of
517   adversarial examples (Entezari et al., 2020; Jin et al., 2020c; Wu et al., 2019a; Kipf & Welling, 2016).
518   Perturbation detection is mostly applied in the pre-processing stage, where adversarial edges or nodes
519   can be identified before training (Xu et al., 2018; Ioannidis et al., 2019; Jin et al., 2020b). Different
520   from them, certified defense is the only approach that secures GNNs theoretically, such that attackers
521   cannot find any adversary to fool the GNNs (Schuchardt et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021; Bojchevski
522   & Günnemann, 2019; Zügner & Günnemann, 2020; Jia et al., 2020). Note that most certified defense
523   approaches only secure the prediction for a specific data point (e.g., a node in node classification).
524   Different from them, ELEGANT enables us to secure the fairness level for GNNs, which naturally
525   entwines with all predictions in the test set.
526
527
528   6    C ONCLUSION
529
530   In this paper, we study a novel problem of certifying GNN node classifiers on fairness. To address
531   this problem, we propose ELEGANT, a framework designed to achieve certification on top of any
532   optimized GNN node classifier associated with certain perturbation budgets, ensuring that it is
533   impossible for attackers to degrade the fairness level of predictions within such budgets. Notably,
534   ELEGANT is designed to serve as a plug-and-play framework for any optimized GNNs and does not
535   rely on any assumptions regarding GNN structures or re-training processes. Extensive experiments
536   verify the strong effectiveness and generalizability of ELEGANT across multiple GNN architectures
537   and real-world datasets. While this paper primarily focuses on the widely studied node classification
538   task, we also highlight the potential for extending this study to other graph-related tasks as a future
539   research direction. We expect positive broader impacts including deploying fairness-safe GNNs in
      applications, and no significant negative broader impact needs to be highlighted here.

760   column; for a vector x, we use x[i] to denote its i-th component.

      A.1   P ROOF OF T HEOREM 1
764
765   To prove Theorem 1, we formulate the theoretical prerequisite that Theorem 1 relies on as Lemma
766   A 1. Similarly, the proof of Lemma A 1 relies on the results in Lemma A 2, and the proof of Lemma
767   A 2 is based on Lemma A 3.
768
769
770
      Proof. For simplification, we reshape the matrix X ∈ Rn×d to the vector x ∈ Rnd . We denote
771
      hc (x) = Pr(g(fθ∗ , A, X + γX (ωX , Vvul ), η, Vtst ) = c) as the function that returns P (c). Without
772
      loss of generality, we assume argmaxc∈{0,1} P (c) = 1, so we have argminc∈{0,1} P (c) = 0 conse-
773
774
      quently. We use ∆X to denote a perturbation on X that satisfies ∆X ≤ ϵ˜X , and ∆x to denote
775   the reshaped vector of ∆X . Denote Φ−1 (·) as the inverse of the standard Gaussian cumulative
776   distribution function. According to Lemma A 1, we have Φ−1 (hc (x)) as a Lipschitz continuous
777   function with a Lipschitz constant of σ1 where σ is the standard deviation of the Gaussian noise ωX .
778   Based on the property of Lipschitz continuous functions, we have
779                                                                         ϵ˜X

781   Correspondingly, we have the following bounds for the output probabilities of class 0 and 1

                                   Φ−1 (h0 (x + ∆x )) − Φ−1 (h0 (x)) ≤          .                        (3)

795                                      h1 (x + ∆x ) ≥ h0 (x + ∆x ).                                     (6)
796
797   In Equation (6), h1 (x + ∆x ) and h0 (x + ∆x ) stand for the output probabilities of class 1 and
798   0 after the perturbation, correspondingly. Hence, the output for g̃X will not change after the
799   perturbation (still class 1). Noting that the exact probabilities maxc∈{0,1} P (c) and minc∈{0,1} P (c)
800   are difficult to calculate in practice, we can use a tractable lower bound pmax and upper bound
801   pmin such that maxc∈{0,1} P (c) ≥ pmax ≥ pmin ≥ minc∈{0,1} P (c) to replace them in ϵ˜X as
802
      ϵ˜X = σ2 (Φ−1 (pmax ) − Φ−1 (pmin )). Because the practical perturbation budget ϵ˜X derived by tractable
803
      bounds is smaller than the true budget, we can still obtain the same result as Equation (6).

      Proof. To certify the fairness level, we assume that g̃A,X (A, X) = 1. Refer to Lemma 2, we have

958                           Pr(g̃X (A ⊕ ∆A ⊕ ΓA , X) = 1) ≥ Pg̃X =1 > 0.5,                       (12)

960   ∥∆A ∥0 ≤ ϵ˜A .

       Proof. Recall that g̃A,X (A, X) = argmaxc∈{0,1} Pr(g̃X (A ⊕ ΓA , X) = c) and g̃X (A, X) =

1050   for any ΓA ∈ Ā where ϵ˜X is derived with classifier g̃X (A ⊕ ΓA , X). Regarding the randomness of
1051   ΓA , we have
1052                Pr(g̃X (A ⊕ ΓA , X + ∆X ) = 1) = Pr(g̃X (A ⊕ ΓA , X) = 1) > 0.5.                 (18)
1053
       Hence we obtain that g̃A,X (A, X + ∆X ) = g̃A,X (A, X) for any perturbation ∥∆X ∥2 ≤ ϵX .
1054
1055
1056   A.8   P ROOF OF P ROPOSITION 1
1057
1058   Proof. For the practical certification, we add perturbations within certified budgets and derive
1059   independent identically distributed output samples Ŷ ′ by Monte Carlo. For each sample Ŷ ′ ∈ Ŷ ′ , we
1060   have Pr(π(Ŷ ′ , Vtst ) > η) < 0.5 according to Theorem 4 and Theorem 2. π(Ŷ , Vtst ) > η indicates
1061   that π(Ŷ ′ , Vtst ) > η for any Ŷ ′ ∈ Ŷ ′ . Consequently, we have
                                                                                                  ′
1062                          Pr(π(Ŷ , Vtst ) > η) = ΠŶ ′ ∈Ŷ ′ Pr(π(Ŷ ′ , Vtst ) > η) < 0.5|Ŷ | .     (19)
1063
1064
1065
1066   A.9   R ATIONALE OF E ACH T HEORETICAL R ESULT
1067
1068   Here we provide an explanation below about the rationale of each theoretical result in this paper.
1069
       Theorem 1. (Certified Fairness Defense for Node Attributes): This theorem gives a way to
1070
       compute the perturbation-invariant budget (i.e., the budget within which the fairness level will not
1071
       reduce under a certain threshold) of node attributes. However, since we consider both input data
1072
1073
       modalities could be attacked, we still need to extend the analysis over the span of node attributes and
1074   graph topology (see Theorem 4).
1075
       Theorem 2. (Certified Defense Budget for Structure Perturbations): This theorem formulates an
1076
       optimization problem, whose solution is the perturbation-invariant budget (i.e., the budget within
1077
       which the fairness level will not reduce under a certain threshold) of graph topology under the
1078
1079
       smoothed node attributes. However, to solve this optimization problem, we need to explicitly
       compute Pg̃X =1 (see Theorem 3).


                                                            20
       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


1080
       Theorem 3. (Positive Probability Lower Bound): This theorem provides a way to explicitly
1081
       compute Pg̃X =1 , which directly enables us to solve the optimization problem in Theorem 2.
1082
1083   Theorem 4. (Certified Defense Budget for Attribute Perturbations): This theorem is built upon
1084   Theorem 1 and provides a way to explicitly compute the perturbation-invariant budget of node
1085   attributes over the span of node attributes and graph topology.
1086
1087   Lemma 1. (Perturbation-Invariant Budgets Existence): This lemma claims the existence and
1088   tractability of the perturbation-invariant budgets on both data modalities, which is further detailed by
1089   Theorem 2 and Theorem 4.
1090
1091   Lemma 2. (Positive Probability Bound Under Noises): This lemma claims the existence and
1092   tractability of Pg̃X =1 , which is further detailed by Theorem 3.
1093
       Proposition 1. (Probabilistic Guarantee for the Fairness Level of Node Classification): This
1094
       proposition provides a neat probabilistic theoretical guarantee — we have a probability that is large
1095
1096
       enough to successfully achieve certified defense on fairness.
1097
1098
1099   B     R EPRODUCIBILITY AND S UPPLEMENTARY A NALYSIS
1100
1101
1102   In this section, our primary emphasis is on ensuring the replicability of our experiments, which serves
1103   as an extension to Section 4. To begin with, we offer a comprehensive introduction of the three
1104   real-world datasets adopted in our experiments. Subsequently, we introduce the detailed experimental
1105   settings, as well as the implementation details of our proposed framework, ELEGANT, alongside
1106   GNNs and baseline models. Moreover, we outline those essential packages, including their versions,
1107   that were utilized in our experiments. Lastly, we elaborate on the supplementary analysis on the time
1108   complexity of ELEGANT.
1109
1110
1111
       B.1    DATASETS
1112
1113
       In our experiments, we adopt three real-world network datasets that are widely used to perform
1114
1115
       studies on the fairness of GNNs, namely German Credit (Asuncion & Newman, 2007; Agarwal et al.,
1116   2021), Recidivism (Jordan & Freiburger, 2015; Agarwal et al., 2021), and Credit Defaulter (Yeh &
1117   Lien, 2009; Agarwal et al., 2021). We introduce their basic information below.
1118
       (1) German Credit. Each node is a client in a German bank (Asuncion & Newman, 2007), while
1119
       each edge between any two clients represents that they bear similar credit accounts. Here the gender
1120
1121
       of bank clients is considered as the sensitive attribute, and the task is to classify the credit risk of the
1122   clients as high or low.
1123
       (2) Recidivism. Each node denotes a defendant released on bail at the U.S state courts during
1124
       1990-2009 (Jordan & Freiburger, 2015), and defendants are connected based on the similarity of their
1125
       past criminal records and demographics. Here the race of defendants is considered as the sensitive
1126
1127
       attribute, and the task is to classify defendants into more likely vs. less likely to commit a violent
1128   crime after being released.
1129
       (3) Credit Defaulter. This dataset contains credit card users collected from financial agencies (Yeh
1130
       & Lien, 2009). Specifically, each node in this network denotes a credit card user, and users are
1131
       connected based on their spending and payment patterns. The sensitive attribute is the age period
1132
1133
       of users, and the task is to predict the future default of credit card for these users. We present the
       statistics pf the three datasets above in Table 2.


                                                           21
       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


1134
1135   Table 2: The statistics and basic information about the six real-world datasets adopted for experimental
1136   evaluation. Sens. represents the semantic meaning of sensitive attribute.
1137
1138
             Dataset                German Credit               Recidivism              Credit Defaulter
1139
1140         # Nodes                     1,000                     18,876                    30,000
1141
1142
             # Edges                     22,242                   321,308                   1,436,858
1143         # Attributes                  27                        18                         13
1144         Avg. degree                  44.5                      34.0                       95.8
1145
             Sens.                      Gender                      Race                       Age
1146
1147         Label                    Credit status            Bail decision              Future default
1148
1149
1150   For the three real-world datasets used in this paper, we adopt the split rate for the training set and
1151
       validation set as 0.4 and 0.55, respectively. The input node features are normalized before they
1152
       are fed into the GNNs and the corresponding explanation models. For the downstream task node
1153
       classification, only the labels of the nodes in the training set is available for all models during the
1154
1155
       training process. The trained GNN models with the best performance on the validation set are
1156   preserved for test and explanation.
1157
1158
       B.2    D ETAILED E XPERIMENTAL S ETTINGS
1159
1160
       Implementation of GNN Models. In our experiments, all GNN models are implemented in Py-
1161
       Torch (Paszke et al., 2017) with PyG (PyTorch Geometric) (Fey & Lenssen, 2019). For the corre-
1162
       sponding hyper-parameters, we set the value of weight decay as 5e-4, with the hidden dimension
1163
1164
       number and dropout rate being 64 and 0.6, respectively. In addition, we set the learning rate and
1165   epoch number as 5e-2 and 200 for training.
1166
       Implementation of ELEGANT. ELEGANT is implemented in PyTorch (Paszke et al., 2017) with
1167
       MIT license and all GNNs under ELEGANT are optimized through Adam optimizer (Kingma & Ba,
1168
       2015) on Nvidia A6000. In our experiments, the sampling sizes of Gaussian noise and Bernoulli
1169
1170
       noise are 150 and 200, respectively. All hyper-parameters for GNNs under ELEGANT are set as the
1171   same values as the hyper-parameters adopted for vanilla GNNs. We propose to add Gaussian and
1172   Bernoulli noise (to node attributes and graph topology) during training, which empirically leads to
1173   better certification performance, i.e., larger certification budgets over both node attributes and graph
1174   topology. Specifically, we set the entry-wise probability of flipping the existence of an edge and the
1175   standard deviation of the added Gaussian noise as 2e-4 and 2e-5, respectively. In addition, we set the
1176   confidence level as 0.7 for estimation, since a lower confidence level helps exhibit a clearer tendency
1177
       of the change of certified budgets w.r.t. other parameters under a limited number of sampling size,
1178
       considering the computational costs. In the test phase, we set the sampled ratio for certification (from
1179
       the nodes out of training and validation set) to be 0.9 to make the sampled size relatively large, in
1180
1181
       which way we include more nodes in the set of nodes to be certified. In each run, we sample 100
1182   times, and the value of FCR is averaged across three runs with different seeds. Finally, considering
1183   the sizes of the three datasets, we set the nodes that are vulnerable to be 5% for German Credit and
1184   1% for others.
1185
       Selection of ϵ and β. There are two critical parameters, ϵ and β, that could affect the effectiveness
1186
1187
       of ELEGANT. These two parameters control the level of randomness for the added Gaussian and
       Bernoulli noise, respectively. Intuitively, larger ϵ and β will induce more randomness in the node


                                                         22
       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


1188
       attributes and graph structure, which could make ELEGANT more robust to perturbations with larger
1189
       sizes and thus achieve larger ϵX and ϵA . However, if ϵX and ϵA are too large, the randomness could
1190
1191
       go beyond what the GNN classifier can manage and could finally cause failure in certification. Hence
1192   it is necessary to first determine appropriate values of ϵX and ϵA for ELEGANT. Here we propose a
1193   strategy for parameter selection to realize as large certified defense budgets as possible. Specifically,
1194   we first set an empirical η to be 25% higher than the fairness level of the corresponding vanilla GNN
1195   model. Such a threshold calibrates across different GNNs and can be considered as a reasonable
1196   threshold for the exhibited bias. Then we determine two wide search spaces for σ and β, respectively,
1197   and compute the averaged ϵX and ϵA from multiple runs over each pair of σ and β values. We now
1198   rank (σ, β) pairs based on the averaged ϵX and ϵA in a descending order, respectively. Finally, we
1199
       truncate the obtained two rankings from their most top-ranked (σ, β) pair to the tail, until the two
1200
       truncated rankings have the first overlapped (σ, β) pair. Such an identified (σ, β) pair can achieve
1201
1202
       large and balanced certification budgets over both A and X, and hence they are recommended.
1203   Implementation of Baseline Models. In this paper, we include two fairness-aware GNNs as the
1204   baselines for comparison, namely FairGNN and NIFTY. We introduce the details below. (1) FairGNN.
1205
       For FairGNN, we adopt the official implementations from (Dai & Wang, 2021). Hyper-parameters
1206
       corresponding to the GNN model structure (such as the number of hidden dimensions) are ensured
1207
1208
       to be the same as the vanilla GNNs for a fair comparison. Other parameters are carefully tuned
1209   under the guidance of the recommended training settings. (2) NIFTY. For NIFTY, we use the official
1210   implementations provided from (Agarwal et al., 2021). We ensured that the parameters related to the
1211   GNN model structure stay the same as the original GNNs for a fair comparison. We also adjust other
1212   parameters based on the suggested training settings for better performance.
1213
1214
       Packages Required for Implementations. We list those key packages and their corresponding
1215   versions adopted in our experiments below.
1216          • Python == 3.8.8
1217
1218          • torch == 1.10.1
1219          • torch-geometric == 2.1.0
1220
1221          • torch-scatter == 2.0.9
1222
              • torch-sparse == 0.6.13
1223
1224          • cuda == 11.1
1225
              • numpy == 1.20.1
1226
1227          • tensorboard == 2.10.0
1228
              • networkx == 2.5
1229
1230          • scikit-learn == 0.24.2
1231
              • pandas==1.2.4
1232
1233          • scipy==1.6.2
1234

       B.3   A LGORITHMIC ROUTINE
1236
1237
       Now we introduce the pipeline of the proposed framework ELEGANT to obtain the node classification
1238
       results in facing of the graph data that could have been perturbed by malicious attackers. We present
1239
       the algorithmic routine in Algorithm 1. Note that ABSTAIN refers to the case where certification fails.
1240
1241
       Correspondingly, FCR measures the ratio of not returning ABSTAIN for the proposed framework
       ELEGANT, which generally reflects the usability of the certification defense.

1316   Algorithm 1 Certified Defense on the Fairness of GNNs
1317    Input:
1318       G: graph data with potential malicious attacks; fθ∗ : an optimized GNN node classifier; Vtrain , Vvalidation ,
1319       Vtest ∈ V: the node set for training, validation, and test, respectively; Vvul ∈ Vtest : the set of vulnerable
1320       nodes that may bear attacks (on node attributes and/or graph topology); N1 , N2 : sample size for the set of
1321       Bernoulli and Gaussian noise, respectively; η: a given threshold for the exhibited bias; α: the parameter
1322       to indicate the confidence level (1 − α) of the estimation; σ: the std of the added Gaussian noise; β: the
1323       probability of returning zero of the added Bernoulli noise;
1324    Output:
1325       ϵA : the certified defense budget over the adjacency matrix A; ϵX : the certified defense budget over the
1326       node attribute matrix X; Ŷ ′ : the output node classification results from the certified classifier;
1327    1: Sample a set of Bernoulli noise QB containing N1 samples;
1328    2: Sample a set of Gaussian noise QG containing N2 samples;
1329    3: for ωA ∈ QB do
1330    4:    for ωX ∈ QG do
1331    5:        Calculate and collect the output of fθ∗ under the noise of ωA and ωX ;
1332    6:        Calculate and collect the output of g based on the output of fθ∗ ;
1333    7:    end for
1334    8:    Under QG , collect the number of g returning 1 and 0 as n1 and n0 , respectively;
1335    9:    Estimate the lower bound of returning c as Pg=c determined by the larger one between n1 and n0 ;
1336   10:    if n1 > n0 and Pg=1 is larger than 0.5 with a confidence level larger than 1 − α or n1 < n0 and Pg=0
1337          is larger than 0.5 with a confidence level larger than 1 − α then
1338   11:        Calculate and collect the value of ϵ̃X ;
1339   12:    else
1340   13:        return ABSTAIN
1341   14:    end if
1342   15: end for
1343   16: Collect the number of cases where n1 > n0 and estimate the lower bound of returning 1 as Pg̃X =1 ;
1344   17: if Pg̃X =1 is larger than 0.5 with a confidence level larger than 1 − α then
1345   18:    Calculate ϵX (out of the collected ϵ̃X ) and ϵA (based on the estimated Pg̃X =1 );
1346   19:    Find Y ′ out of the collected output of fθ∗ ;
1347   20:    return Y ′ , ϵX , and ϵA ;
1348   21: else
1349   22:    return ABSTAIN
       23: end if


                                                               25
       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


1350
       B.6    O RDERING THE I NNER AND O UTER D EFENSE
1351
1352
       We first review the general pipeline to achieve certified fairness defense. Specifically, we first model
1353
       the fairness attack and defense by formulating the bias indicator function g. Then, we achieve certified
1354
1355
       defense over the node attributes for g, which leads to classifier g̃X . Finally, we realize certified de-
1356   fense for g̃X over the graph topology, which leads to classifier g̃A,X . In general, we may consider the
1357   certified defense over node attributes and graph topology as the inner certified classifier and outer cer-
1358   tified classifier, respectively. Now, a natural question is: is it possible to achieve certified defense in a
1359   different order, i.e., first achieve certified defense over the graph topology (as the inner classifier), and
1360   then realize certified defense over the node attributes (as the outer classifier)? Note that this is not the
1361   research focus of this paper, but we will provide insights about this question. In fact, it is also feasible
1362   to achieve certified defense in the reversed order compared with the approach presented in our paper.
1363
       We provide an illustration in Figure 5. We follow a similar setting to plot this figure as in Section 3.3.
1364
       Specifically, in case (1), both Ai,j ⊕ 0 and Ai,j ⊕ 1 lead to a positive outcome for g; in case (2), both
1365
1366
       Ai,j ⊕ 0 and Ai,j ⊕ 1 lead to a negative outcome. However, considering the Gaussian distribution
1367   around Xi,j , samples will fall around case (1) with a much higher number compared with case (2).
1368   Hence, in this example, it would be reasonable to
       assume that the classifier with Bernoulli noise over                             ty
1369
                                                                                    bili
       graph topology (the inner certified classifier) will                       ba      n
                                                                                ro nctio
1370
       return 1 with a higher probability. This example                       P
1371
                                                                            of F u
1372   thus illustrates how certification following a different          lue sity                    t: 1
                                                                        a
                                                                      V en                        tpu
1373   order returns 1.                                                   D                     ou (1)
1374                                                                                        ner            (2)
                                                                                         In
1375   However, such a formulation bears higher computa-
1376   tional costs in calculating the certified budgets. The
1377   reason is that we are able to utilize a closed-form solu-
1378   tion to calculate ϵX based on a set of Gaussian noise


                                                                                                              0
                                                                                                           t:
1379   and the corresponding output from the bias indicator


                                                                                                          pu
                                                                                                        ut
1380
       function. However, based on a set of Bernoulli noise
                                                                                                      ro
1381                                                                                                ne
       and the corresponding output from the bias indica-
                                                                                                  In
1382
1383
       tor function, we will need to solve the optimization
1384   problem given in Theorem 2 to calculate ϵA , which
1385   bears a higher time complexity than calculating ϵX . Figure 5: An example illustrating how ELE-
1386   If we follow the strategy provided in Section 3.4 to GANT works with a different order to achieve
1387   calculate the inner and outer certification budgets, the certified defense.
1388   certified budget of the inner certification will always
1389   be calculated multiple times, while the certified budget of the outer certification will only be calcu-
1390   lated once. Considering the high computational cost of calculating ϵA , we thus argue that it is more
1391
       efficient to realize the certification over graph topology as the outer certified classifier.
1392
1393
1394   B.7    C ERTIFICATION WITH E STIMATED P ROBABILITIES
1395
1396   In Section 3.4, we proposed to utilize estimated lower bounds of the probabilities (including P (c) in
1397   Theorem 1 and Pr(g̃X (A ⊕ ΓA , X) = 1) in Theorem 3) to perform certification in practice, consid-
1398   ering the exact probability values are difficult to compute. In Appendix A.1 and Appendix A.5, we
1399
       have discussed that both theorems hold no matter exact probability values or estimated lower bounds
1400
       (of the probabilities above) are used. Now we present a brief review of other theoretical analysis
1401
       to show that they also hold. (1) for Lemma 2, note that taking a lower bound estimation to replace
1402
1403
       the exact Pr(g̃X (A ⊕ ΓA , X) = 1) reduces the total size of H in Theorem 3. Correspondingly, the
       formulated Pg̃X =1 based on the estimated Pr(g̃X (A ⊕ ΓA , X) = 1) is smaller than that based on


                                                           26
       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


1404
       the exact Pr(g̃X (A ⊕ ΓA , X) = 1). Hence Lemma 2 still holds when Pg̃X =1 is replaced with one
1405
       calculated based on the estimated Pr(g̃X (A ⊕ ΓA , X) = 1). (2) For Theorem 2, it holds no matter
1406
1407
       how P (c) in Theorem 1 and Pr(g̃X (A ⊕ ΓA , X) = 1) in Theorem 3 are obtained. (3) For Theorem
1408   4, according to Appendix A.7, it still holds as long as Theorem 1 holds. (4) For Proposition 1, in
1409   all cases where Pr(g(A ⊕ Γ′A , X + ΓX ) = 1) is identified to be larger than 0.5 with an estimated
1410   lower bound, the (underlying) exact Pr(g(A ⊕ Γ′A , X + ΓX ) = 1) will be larger than the estimated
1411   probability value under the given confidence level, and thus will also be larger than 0.5. Here Γ′A is a
1412   sampled Bernoulli noise, i.e., Γ′A ∈ Ā′ . According to Appendix A.8, Proposition 1 still holds in this
1413   case. (5) Finally, we conclude that Lemma 1 still holds since Theorem 1, Lemma 2, Theorem 3, and
1414   Theorem 4 hold.
1415
1416
1417
       B.8   T IME C OMPLEXITY A NALYSIS
1418
       We now present a comprehensive analysis on the time complexity of ELEGANT. We present the
1419
1420
       analysis from both theoretical and experimental perspectives.
1421   Theoretical. The time complexity is linear w.r.t. the total number of the random perturbations N , i.e.,
1422   O(N ). We perform 30,000 random perturbations over the span of node attributes and graph structure.
1423
       We note that the actual running time is acceptable since the certification does not require re-training
1424
       (which is the most costly process). In addition, all runnings do not rely on the prediction results from
1425
1426
       each other. Hence they can be paralleled altogether theoretically to further reduce the running time.
1427   Experimental. We perform a study of running time, and we present the results in Table 4. Specifically,
1428   we compare the running time of a successful certification under 30,000 random noise samples and
1429
       a regular training-inference cycle with vanilla GCN. We observe that (1) although ELEGANT
1430
       improves the computational cost compared with the vanilla GNN backbones, the running time
1431
1432
       remains acceptable; and (2) ELEGANT has less running time growth rate on larger datasets. For
1433   example, E-SAGE has around 10x running time on German Credit (a smaller dataset) while only
1434   around 4x on Credit Default (a larger dataset) compared to vanilla SAGE. Hence we argue that
1435   ELEGANT bears a high level of usability in terms of complexity and running time.
1436
1437   B.9   A DDITIONAL R ESULTS ON D IFFERENT GNN BACKBONES & BASELINES
1438
1439   We perform additional experiments over two popular GNNs, including APPNP (Klicpera et al., 2019)
1440   and GCNII (Chen et al., 2020), to evaluate the generalization ability of ELEGANT onto different
1441
       backbones. We present all numerical results in Table 5 (in terms of accuracy), Table 6 (in terms of
1442
       fairness), and Table 7 (in terms of FCR). We observe that ELEGANT achieves comparable utility,
1443
       a superior level of fairness, and a large percentage of FCR. This verifies the satisfying usability of
1444
1445
       ELEGANT, which remains consistent with the paper.
1446   In addition, we provide a detailed fairness comparison between ELEGANT and robust GNNs from
1447
       (Jin et al., 2021) and (Wu et al., 2019b) in Table 8. We observe that the best performances still come
1448
       from the GNNs equipped with ELEGANT on all datasets. Hence we argue that ELEGANT exhibits
1449
       satisfying performance in usability, which remains consistent with the discussion in the paper.
1450
1451   Why ELEGANT Improves Fairness? We note that improving fairness is a byproduct of ELEGANT,
1452   and our focus is to achieve certification over the fairness level of the prediction results. We now
1453
       provide a detailed discussion about why fairness is improved here. First, existing works found that
1454
       the distribution difference in the node attribute values and edge existence across different subgroups
1455
       is a significant source of bias (Dong et al., 2022a; Dai & Wang, 2021; Fan et al., 2021). However,
1456
1457
       adding noise on both node attributes and graph topology may reduce such distributional divergence
       and mitigate bias. Second, As mentioned in Section 3.4, the proposed strategy to obtain the output


                                                         27
       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


1458
       predictions in ELEGANT is to select the fairest result among the output set Ŷ ′ , where each output
1459
       is derived based on a sample Γ′A ∈ Ā′ (i.e., argminŶ ′ π(Ŷ ′ , Vtst ) s.t. Ŷ ′ ∈ Ŷ ′ ). Such a strategy
1460
1461
       provides a large enough probability to achieve certification in light of Proposition 1. Meanwhile, we
1462   point out that such a strategy also helps to significantly improve fairness since highly biased outputs
1463   are excluded.
1464

1469   ELEGANT does not constantly show a lower value of ∆EO . This is because the certification goal
1470   in Table 1 is ∆SP instead of ∆EO . In addition, we note that debiasing existing GNN models is not
1471   the goal of this paper. In addition, we provide the corresponding results in terms of accuracy for
1472
       Figure 3 in Table 14 and Table 15. We observe that although most performance remains stable, a
1473
       stronger noise (i.e., larger σ and smaller β) generally leads to worse but still comparable performance.
1474
1475
       This is consistent with the discussion in Section 4.4, and this has been taken into consideration in the
1476   discussion of the parameter selection strategy in Section 4.4.

1510
1511
       We note that the rationale of certified defense is to provably maintain the classification results against
       attacks. Under this context, most existing works on certifying an existing deep learning model focus

1539
1540
1541
1542
       on certifying a specific predicted label over a given data point. Here, the prediction results to be
1543
       certified are classification results. Correspondingly, these works are able to certify the model itself.
1544
1545   However, the strategy above is not feasible in our studied problem. This is because we seek to
1546   certify the level of fairness of a group of nodes. The value of such a group-level property cannot be
1547   directly considered as a classification result, and thus they are not feasible to be directly certified.
1548
       Therefore, we proposed to first formulate a classifier on top of an optimized GNN. As such, achieving
1549
       certification becomes feasible. In fact, this also serves as one of the contributions of our work.
1550
1551
1552
1553   C.2   W HAT I S THE D IFFERENCE B ETWEEN THE ATTACKING P ERFORMANCE OF GNN S AND
1554
             THE FAIRNESS OF GNN S ?
1555
1556
1557   In traditional attacks over the performance of GNNs, the objective of the attacker is simply formulated
1558   as having false predictions on as many nodes as possible, such that the overall performance is
1559   jeopardized. However, in attacks over the fairness of GNNs, whether the goal of the attacker can be
1560   achieved is jointly determined by the GNN predictions over all nodes. Such node-level dependency in
1561
       achieving the attacking goal makes the defense over fairness attacks more difficult, since the defense
1562
       cannot be directly performed at the node level but at the model level instead. Correspondingly,
1563
       this necessitates (1) constructing an additional classifier as discussed in the previous reply, and (2)
1564
1565
       additional theoretical analysis over the constructed classifier as in Theorem 1, 2, and 3 to achieve
       certification.


                                                         29
       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025

1592   C.3     C ERTIFICATION W ITHOUT C ONSIDERING THE B INARY S ENSITIVE ATTRIBUTE
1593
1594   We utilize the most widely studied setting to assume the sensitive attributes are binary. However, our
1595
       certification approach is not designed to be tailored to the sensitive attributes. Therefore, our approach
1596
       can be easily extended to scenarios where the sensitive attributes are multi-class and continuous by
1597
1598
       adopting the corresponding fairness metric as the function π(·) in Definition 1.
1599
1600
       C.4     H OW D O THE M AIN T HEORETICAL F INDINGS D IFFER F ROM E XISTING W ORKS ON
1601
               ROBUSTNESS C ERTIFICATION OF GNN S ON R EGULAR ATTACKS ?
1602
1603
       Most existing works for robustness certification can only defend against attacks on either node
1604
1605   attributes or graph structure. Due to the multi-modal input data of GNNs, existing works usually fail
1606   to handle the attacks over node attributes and graph structure at the same time. However, ELEGANT
1607   is able to defend against attacks over both data modalities. This necessitates using both continuous
1608   and discrete noises for smoothing and the analysis for joint certification in the span of the two input
1609   data modalities (as shown in Figure 1).
1610
1611

1639
1640   C.5    D ISCUSSION : D IFFERENCE WITH E XISTING S IMILAR W ORKS
1641
1642   Here we mainly focus on discussing the difference between this work and (Bojchevski et al., 2020).
1643
       We note that (1) the randomized smoothing technique adopted in (Bojchevski et al., 2020) is different
1644
       from the proposed randomized smoothing approach on the graph topology in this paper and (2) the
1645
1646   techniques in (Bojchevski et al., 2020) tackle a different problem from this paper. We elaborate on
1647   more details below.
1648
       The techniques in (Bojchevski et al., 2020) are different from this paper. Although both randomized
1649
       smoothing approaches are able to handle binary data, we note that the randomized smoothing
1650
       approach proposed in (Bojchevski et al., 2020) is data-dependent. However, the proposed randomized
1651
1652   smoothing approach in this paper is data-independent. We note that in practice, a data-independent
1653   approach enables practitioners to pre-generate noises, which significantly improves usability.
1654
       The studied problem in (Bojchevski et al., 2020) is different from this paper. Although the authors
1655
       claimed to achieve a joint certificate for graph topology and node attributes in (Bojchevski et al.,
1656
       2020), all node attributes are assumed to be binary, which can only be applied to cases where these
1657
1658   attributes are constructed as bag-of-words representations (as mentioned in the second last paragraph
1659   in the Introduction of (Bojchevski et al., 2020)). However, in this work, we follow a more realistic
1660   setting where only graph topology is assumed to be binary while node attributes are considered as
1661   continuous. This makes the problem more difficult to handle, since different strategies should be
1662   adopted for different data modalities. In summary, compared with (Bojchevski et al., 2020), the
1663   problem studied in this paper is more realistic and more suitable for GNNs.
1664
1665
1666   Table 13: The results under (23 , 102 ) in terms of node classification accuracy, AUC score, F1 score,
1667   ∆SP , and ∆EO for Figure 2. All numerical numbers are in percentage.
1668
1669
             (23 , 102 )      Accuracy           AUC            F1 Score           ∆SP            ∆EO

       Based on the discussion above, we would like to note that no existing work can be directly adopted to
1701   tackle the studied problem in this paper.
1702
1703   C.6    A DDITIONAL E XPERIMENTS ON D IFFERENT DATASETS
1704
1705   To further validate the performance of the proposed method, we also perform experiments with the
1706   same commonly used popular GNN backbone models (as Section 4.2) on two Pokec datasets, namely
1707   Pokec-z and Pokec-n. We present the experimental results in Table 16, where all numerical numbers
1708   are in percentage. We observe that (1) the GNNs equipped with ELEGANT achieve comparable
1709   node classification accuracy; (2) the GNNs equipped with ELEGANT achieve consistently lower
1710
       levels of bias; and (3) the values of the Fairness Certification Rate (FCR) for all GNNs equipped with
1711
       ELEGANT exceed 90%, exhibiting satisfying usability. All three observations are consistent with
1712
       the experimental results and the corresponding discussion presented in Section 4.2. Therefore, we
1713
1714   argue that the effectiveness of the proposed approach is not determined by the dataset and is well
1715   generalizable over different graph datasets.
1716
1717   C.7    S CALABILITY OF ELEGANT
1718
1719   In this subsection, we discuss the scalability of ELEGANT. Specifically, we note that if the Gaussian
1720   and Bernoulli noise is directly added over the whole graph, scaling to larger graphs would be difficult.
1721   However, the proposed approach can be easily extended to the batch training case, which has been
1722   widely adopted by existing scalable GNNs. Specifically, a commonly adopted batch training strategy
1723
       of scalable GNNs is to only input a node and its surrounding subgraph into the GNN, since the
1724
       prediction of GNNs only depends on the information of the node itself and its multi-hop neighbors,
1725
       and the number of hops is determined by the layer number of GNNs. Since the approach proposed in
1726
1727
       our paper aligns with the basic pipeline of GNNs, the perturbation can also be performed for each
       specific batch of nodes. In this case, all theoretical analyses in this paper still hold, since they also do

       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025
